validity_gate.justification: The aggregate task generates 600 World-Swap problems and calls llm.prompt(problem_text + suffix, schema=UnifiedResp) on each via _unified_infer, then scores the model's actual structured responses (is_correct, confidence, slop) and aggregates into SDT/decision-theoretic metrics. A real model (claude-opus-4-7) was run, scoring 462/600.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Ground truth (correct_answer/naive_answer) is generated deterministically per problem and _classify_result uses substring + multi-layer slop detection, but matching is fragile: is_correct relies on bare substring containment (cn in ma or ma in cn) with no numeric tolerance for arithmetic and no anchored rubric, so a verbose answer containing the naive number could mis-score. Verification exists for all items but has rough edges.

2_construct_validity.justification: The design tightly isolates metacognition: counterfactual World-Swap rules force suppression of priors (inhibitory control), Tier 1 identity-rule items use scaffold-equalized prompts to remove the scaffolding confound, and paired correct/naive answers plus confidence let it separate ordinary error, semantic slop, miscalibration, and tool misuse. Deliberate controls (TIER1_PROMPT mirrors UNIFIED_PROMPT) directly target the claimed construct.

3_code_quality.justification: Clean, modular, well-sectioned code using the SDK idiomatically (@kbench.task, Parallel inference, enable_cache, schema-constrained outputs), with robust handling: _safe_call wraps every trial, sanitize_for_protobuf guards NaN/inf, confidence normalization, fallback slop layers, and embedding-model load wrapped in try/except. Some bare except blocks exist but overall hardened.

4_sample_size.justification: Generates N=600 evaluation items (150 per tier x 4 tiers, balanced across 4 problem types), far exceeding the 20-item threshold and enabling per-tier and per-type analysis.

5_metric_design.justification: Computes many distinct dimensions beyond accuracy: meta-d'/d' (SDT metacognitive efficiency), ECE, ACE (asymmetric Brier), selective-prediction score, tool utility, confidence entropy, plus bootstrap 95% CIs. Calibration vs accuracy are separate dimensions and markdown justifies the SDT/decision-theoretic framing.

6_problem_statement.justification: The notebook header names the capability (metacognition, SDT meta-d'/d', four failure modes) and lays out the four tasks and dataset, giving clear motivation, but it does not connect to a broader research question or articulate the gap relative to existing metacognition benchmarks. No separate task-page text is provided beyond the embedded header.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Notebook has section headers and docstrings describing the engine, slop detection, metrics, and prompts, and metrics are interpretable. However there is no dataset-schema walkthrough, edge-case scoring rules, or step-by-step methodology narrative in markdown; documentation is partial and code-reading is required to reproduce.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes a specific sub-capability (metacognitive monitoring under prepotent-prior interference) with an explicit hypothesis and experimental controls: 4 difficulty tiers (identity-rule baseline vs increasing swaps), the InverseScalingIndex (T1 acc minus T4 acc), and InhibitoryAccuracy on swapped tiers allow attributing performance to inhibition/calibration rather than confounds.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are explicitly organized into a structured taxonomy: 4 difficulty tiers x 4 problem types (operator_swap, physics, linguistic, logic), and the evaluation reports accuracy/confidence broken down per tier (Tier1-4Acc/Conf) and per failure mode, revealing where models fail across a performance gradient.

10_model_coverage.justification: Only one model run is present (claude-opus-4-7@default, run_id Run_1); no second model's results are included, so no cross-model comparison is possible.

validity_gate.justification: The two notebooks chosen for the leaderboard fail to measure a model: temporalbenchv4 and temporalbench-v2-pccc build an internal TemporalAttentionStore, call store.get(), and assert predicted is not None (e.g. '942/942', '734/734', '324/324 correct') while ignoring the llm parameter entirely -- the gate's exact example of testing the authors' own data structure. The temporalbench-eval notebook does call llm.prompt() but its sole executed task (v4 OverlapQuery) is fundamentally broken, scoring 0.0/734 for all items because expected answers are opaque version codes (e.g. 'v26') the score_answer harness can never match. task-2-temporal-ambiguity genuinely prompts a model over 2000 items but the run FAILED on a 403 quota error producing no score. The only notebook that genuinely scores a live model (staleness) runs on 3 hardcoded mock questions. No primary/headline evaluation ever produces a valid model score, so the gate fails.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Forced to Level 0 by the validity-gate failure. Additionally, the store-only notebooks assert merely that a value is non-None (never comparing to ground truth), and the live-model v4 task scored 0/734 due to an answer-format mismatch with opaque version codes.

2_construct_validity.justification: Forced to Level 0 by the validity-gate failure. The leaderboard notebooks claim to measure LLM temporal reasoning but actually exercise the authors' retrieval store (no model) or fail entirely (0/734), so there is a clear mismatch between stated purpose and what is measured.

3_code_quality.justification: Code is functional and somewhat organized (dataclasses, store classes, normalize_fact, replay_events) but has serious defects: the eval notebook swallows all errors with bare except: pass, the v4 task silently yields 0/734, and two notebooks pass llm but never use it. The staleness notebook substitutes a MockStore for real data.

4_sample_size.justification: The underlying dataset is large (2000 questions per version/seed; the v4 OverlapQuery task ran 734 items, ambiguity task 2000 items), well above the 20-item threshold -- though the staleness notebook only uses 3 mock items.

5_metric_design.justification: Only a single metric (accuracy via substring/token-overlap score_answer, or pass/fail assertions) is computed, with no calibration, latency, or second dimension and no justification; for the store-only notebooks the 'metric' is just non-None presence.

6_problem_statement.justification: The header names the capability (temporal reasoning: validity windows vs decay, as-of/staleness/overlap queries) and gives basic motivation and task families, but does not connect to a broader research question or identify the gap relative to existing temporal benchmarks.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Partial documentation: the dataset path/structure and task descriptions are stated and the data schema (questions/facts/events with as_of_day, valid_from/until) is partly inferable, but scoring rules, the version-code answer format, and edge cases are undocumented, and the key_findings block is hardcoded rather than computed.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Targets the right domain (temporal point-in-time reasoning) with named task families (AsOfQA, PastQueryTrap, OverlapQuery, ChangeDetection, DecayTrap), but the implementation does not isolate the capability in a model -- controls live inside the retrieval store rather than in model-facing items -- so it does not cleanly attribute performance to a model's temporal reasoning.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items carry task_family and version labels (v1-v4 tiers, multiple families) giving natural variation, but the executed evaluations do not report results broken down by these sub-conditions (the v4 task reports a single 0/734 aggregate), so no structured performance gradient is surfaced.

10_model_coverage.justification: Across notebooks, runs reference distinct models (zai_glm-5 for v4 OverlapQuery, anthropic claude-opus-4-7 for PastQueryTrap, google/gemini-2.5-flash for the ambiguity attempt) -- 2-3 distinct models appear, though no single task was validly run across all of them.
